content.text: What It Is

Disclosure timelines and remediation SLAs are the contractual commitments embedded in a Vulnerability Disclosure Programme (VDP) that govern how quickly the organisation will acknowledge, assess, fix, and allow public disclosure of reported vulnerabilities. They convert the VDP from a general promise of good-faith engagement into specific, measurable obligations that the organisation takes on toward reporting researchers and, implicitly, toward the users and customers whose security depends on timely remediation. Without defined timelines, the coordination process between organisation and researcher is undefined — a researcher who has not received a response two months after reporting a Critical vulnerability has no basis on which to judge whether the organisation is remediating or ignoring the finding, and may reasonably decide to disclose publicly.

The timelines operate on two dimensions. The remediation SLA defines how long the organisation has to fix a validated vulnerability before the researcher may disclose it publicly — the internal deadline. The disclosure coordination window defines how long after the fix is deployed the two parties agree to wait before publishing a joint advisory — typically to allow for patch propagation and to avoid providing attackers with a roadmap to exploit a window between announcement and user deployment. For agentic AI systems, the remediation timeline must account for the additional complexity of AI-specific fixes: prompt hardening, architectural changes to agent tool access, or orchestration layer authentication changes that require more extensive testing than a traditional code patch.
Why It Matters

Defined disclosure timelines create accountability. When SLAs are published in the VDP policy, missed deadlines are visible and consequential: the researcher has a basis to escalate to public disclosure, the organisation's credibility in the security community is at stake, and regulatory frameworks that reference coordinated disclosure practices have an expectation of timely remediation. Organisations that consistently miss stated SLAs quickly develop a reputation that deters high-quality researchers from engaging with their programme.

Internally, published SLAs create engineering priority pressure. A Critical vulnerability with a 7-day fix SLA must be escalated to emergency engineering resources regardless of other priorities — the published commitment creates a forcing function that internal severity ratings without external deadlines often do not achieve. Security teams in mature organisations routinely cite the external SLA commitment as the mechanism that gets Critical security fixes the engineering resource allocation they need.
Benefits
Researcher trust:

Published SLAs demonstrate commitment rather than intent. Researchers who know they will receive a response within a defined window are more likely to coordinate through the VDP rather than disclose without engagement.
Engineering prioritisation:

External remediation deadlines create priority signals that pure internal severity ratings often do not. A published 7-day Critical SLA commands engineering resource allocation that 'high priority backlog' does not.
User protection:
Faster remediation of validated vulnerabilities reduces the window during which users are exposed to exploitable weaknesses, particularly important for agentic systems with broad tool access.
Regulatory alignment:

SLA commitments that align with industry norms (7/30/90/180 days for Critical/High/Medium/Low) position the organisation favourably in regulatory assessments of its vulnerability management practices.
Reduced uncoordinated disclosure risk:

Researchers who have received an acknowledgement and a clear timeline are far less likely to go public before the fix is ready; researchers with no information are likely to disclose after a period of silence.
Trade-offs and Limitations
AI remediation complexity:

Prompt injection and model-level vulnerability fixes are often not simple code patches. A 7-day Critical SLA that works for web application vulnerabilities may be unrealistic for an agentic AI system where the fix requires architectural changes to the orchestration layer and new safety evaluation runs against the updated system.
SLA miss consequences:

Publishing SLAs you cannot consistently meet damages credibility more than never having published them. The commitment is a double-edged instrument: honoured, it builds trust; missed, it invites public disclosure and reputational damage.
Scope of the fix:

An organisation and researcher may disagree on whether the deployed fix fully addresses the root cause. If the organisation claims the SLA is met but the researcher believes the fix is incomplete, the disclosure coordination process breaks down, requiring a dispute resolution mechanism in the policy.
Extension management:

Extensions to remediation timelines require researcher consent and must be used sparingly. A programme that routinely requests extensions loses the credibility that the original SLA commitment established.
How It Works

The disclosure timeline is structured as a series of nested deadlines from the date of initial report submission. The acknowledgement deadline is the first commitment: the organisation must confirm receipt of the report within the stated window. This is the easiest SLA to meet and one of the most important: a researcher who receives no acknowledgement within a reasonable window has no basis for confidence that the report was received.

The verdict deadline defines how long the organisation has to validate or reject the report (determine scope and reproducibility) and communicate the outcome to the researcher. This commitment drives the triage process: triage must be completed within the verdict SLA to allow the communication to go out on time. For Critical findings, the verdict window is short — 48 hours — because the researcher needs to know quickly whether the finding is being treated as an emergency. For Medium and Low findings, the verdict window can be longer because the urgency of the finding does not justify the same triage intensity.

The remediation deadline defines the fix target — how many days from the date of validation the organisation commits to deploying a fix. This is the most consequential commitment, because missing it gives the researcher the right to disclose publicly after giving additional notice. For agentic AI systems, the remediation deadline for Critical findings should reflect the actual engineering complexity of AI security fixes: a 7-day target is achievable for configuration-level fixes (disabling a tool integration, adding an authentication check) but may require an extension request for architectural changes (redesigning the agent's tool permission model, implementing a safety evaluation pipeline).

The disclosure coordination window is the period after fix deployment during which the two parties coordinate the joint advisory. This window allows the organisation to prepare its security advisory, the researcher to prepare their write-up, and both parties to agree on the content and timing of publication. The maximum disclosure window — the point at which the researcher may disclose even if coordinated disclosure has not been completed — prevents the organisation from using the coordination phase to indefinitely delay public disclosure after a fix has been deployed.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Disclosure timeline table showing ACK, verdict, fix target, and maximum disclosure windows by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low), plus extension policy

content.text: The four-tier SLA structure ensures that the most urgent findings receive emergency engineering attention (Critical: 7-day fix) while lower-severity findings are addressed within realistic engineering cycles (Low: 180-day fix). All tiers share a consistent post-fix disclosure coordination window of 14-30 days, after which the researcher may publish regardless of whether coordinated advisory drafting is complete.
When to Use It

Disclosure timelines must be defined and published before the VDP accepts its first report. The timelines cannot be set retroactively: a researcher who submits a report without a stated SLA has no framework within which to evaluate whether they are receiving timely treatment, and the organisation has no documented commitment to measure itself against.

Organisations that cannot realistically meet standard industry SLAs for agentic AI vulnerabilities (because of the additional complexity of AI security fixes) should publish more conservative timelines that they can honour, rather than copying standard software VDP timelines that they may miss. A Critical SLA of 14 days that is consistently honoured is more valuable for researcher trust than a 7-day SLA with a consistent request for extension.
How to Implement
Assess engineering capacity for AI security fixes:

Before setting SLA targets, assess how long it actually takes to develop, test, and deploy fixes for the AI vulnerability classes in scope. Run this assessment on a representative set of vulnerability types — prompt injection fix, orchestration auth patch, context isolation fix — to calibrate realistic timelines.
Set SLAs based on measured capacity, not aspiration:

Use the capacity assessment to set SLAs that represent 90th-percentile delivery capability, not best-case. The SLA should be achievable under normal operational load, not only when a Critical finding is given emergency resource allocation.
Define the extension policy:

Publish maximum extension durations for each severity tier and the process for requesting an extension. Extensions should require written notice to the researcher, a revised target date, and a rationale. Limit extensions to one per finding unless extraordinary circumstances exist.
Define researcher rights at deadline miss:

State explicitly in the policy what happens if the organisation misses the remediation deadline: the researcher must give a defined notice period (typically 7 days) before publishing. This protects both parties: the researcher has clear rights; the organisation has a final chance to expedite.
Integrate SLAs into the case management system:
Configure the VDP platform to track SLA due dates for each milestone and alert the responsible team member when a deadline is approaching (at 75% and 90% of the SLA window) and when it is breached.
Define the advisory coordination process:

Document the steps for coordinating the joint advisory: who drafts, who reviews, what the researcher can publish about their own work, how CVE assignment works, and the maximum post-fix disclosure window.
Review SLA performance quarterly:

Track SLA compliance by severity tier and report trends to the security leadership team. If SLA miss rates for any tier exceed 10%, the root cause must be identified — whether insufficient engineering capacity, unclear fix ownership, or unrealistic SLA commitments — and addressed.
Examples
A complete SLA tracking implementation for a VDP case:
Real-World Example

A company receives a Critical vulnerability report describing a prompt injection in its agentic AI customer service bot. The bot has access to the company's CRM system and can query and update customer records. The injection allows an attacker who controls a chat message to cause the bot to execute arbitrary CRM queries and return customer PII in the chat response.

The triage team validates the finding within 36 hours and sends the researcher a severity confirmation with the 7-day fix target. The security lead escalates to the engineering team with a P0 priority tag. The engineering team assesses the fix options: a short-term fix (disabling the CRM write access until a proper validation layer is built) and a long-term fix (adding structured output validation to all CRM tool calls and implementing a new confirmation layer for queries that return PII).

On day 5, the short-term fix (disabling write access and restricting read queries to non-PII fields) is deployed and validated against the researcher's proof-of-concept. The researcher confirms the original vector is mitigated. The security team communicates that the fix for the root cause will be deployed within 30 additional days, and requests the researcher agree to extend the disclosure coordination window to 30 days post-complete-fix. The researcher agrees. On day 32, the complete fix is deployed. Coordinated disclosure — security advisory from the company, researcher write-up — is published on day 46. The researcher is credited by name (with their consent), the advisory includes a CVE number, and the OWASP reference for indirect prompt injection.
Common Mistakes
Copying traditional software SLAs without AI adjustment:

A 7-day Critical SLA for a web application patch may be realistic for a code fix but unrealistic for an architectural change to an agentic system's tool permission model. Calibrate SLAs to actual AI engineering velocity.
No extension policy:

Publishing SLAs without an extension process leaves the organisation without a legitimate option when a fix takes longer than expected, forcing a choice between missing the stated deadline and deploying an incomplete fix.
Ambiguous fix definition:

Claiming the fix SLA is met when only a temporary mitigation is deployed, without informing the researcher, damages the relationship and may result in disclosure of a vulnerability that is not yet fully remediated.
Indefinite post-fix disclosure coordination:

Without a maximum post-fix disclosure window, the organisation can deploy a fix and then delay advisory publication indefinitely. This prevents researchers from publishing their work and is widely considered bad faith in the security community.
No SLA tracking in the case system:
Relying on the triage engineer to remember SLA due dates without system-level tracking results in missed deadlines that are not caught until the researcher follows up.
Best Practices

Publish SLAs you have verified you can meet. Calibrate against your actual engineering velocity for AI security fixes, not against industry standards that may not reflect the complexity of agentic system remediation.

Separate short-term mitigation from root-cause fix in your SLA tracking. Communicating a two-stage fix plan to the researcher — immediate mitigation deployed on day X, complete root-cause fix on day Y — is transparent and allows SLA management to reflect the actual fix trajectory.

Grant extensions proactively when they are needed, not reactively after a deadline is missed. Reaching out to the researcher with a revised timeline and rationale before the deadline maintains trust; waiting until after a miss is damaging.

Define and publish the researcher's rights at deadline miss. This is not a threat — it is a commitment that the organisation takes vulnerability disclosure seriously enough to give researchers legitimate recourse if remediation stalls.

Allow researchers to validate that the fix is complete before the post-fix disclosure window begins. This prevents disputes about whether the SLA has been met and gives the researcher ownership in confirming resolution.
Performance and Scalability

SLA compliance performance should be tracked as a quantitative metric and reported quarterly to security leadership. The key metrics are: acknowledgement SLA compliance rate (% of reports acknowledged within the stated window), verdict SLA compliance rate, fix SLA compliance rate by severity tier, and extension rate (% of validated findings requiring an extension). An extension rate above 20% for any severity tier indicates the SLA for that tier is miscalibrated relative to engineering capacity and should be revised.

For agentic AI systems that are evolving rapidly — with new tool integrations added frequently and model updates changing the attack surface — SLA targets should be reviewed when the system's complexity increases significantly. A Critical SLA that was achievable when the agent had three tool integrations may need revision when it has fifteen, because the fix complexity and blast radius assessment are materially different.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Tool
Role in SLA Management
HackerOne / Bugcrowd
Built-in SLA tracking per severity; dashboard showing overdue reports; automated alerts to responsible engineers
Jira / Linear
Engineering remediation ticket with SLA due date field; integration with VDP platform for automatic ticket creation on validation
PagerDuty / OpsGenie
Critical and High SLA breach alerts to on-call security engineering; escalation to engineering management on missed deadline
MITRE CVE Programme
CVE assignment coordination for published vulnerabilities; CNA registration for organisations wishing to issue their own CVEs

Security and Reliability

The fix SLA for Critical agentic AI vulnerabilities must be tracked with the same urgency as a production incident. A Critical finding with a 7-day fix SLA should trigger on-call escalation immediately upon validation — not be queued for the next sprint. The engineering team must have an established process for emergency security fixes that operates outside the normal sprint cycle for Critical findings.

For agentic AI systems, additional reliability consideration applies to the fix itself: a hasty patch to a safety-critical component of the orchestration layer may introduce new vulnerabilities or break existing safety controls. The fix testing protocol must include safety regression testing — verifying that the agentic system's established safety guardrails are intact after the fix is applied — before the fix is deployed and the SLA is considered met.
Key Takeaways
Disclosure timelines convert the VDP from a promise of good-faith engagement into specific, measurable commitments that create accountability for both the organisation and the researcher.

SLAs must be calibrated to the actual engineering velocity for AI security fixes, not copied from traditional software VDP templates. Agentic AI vulnerabilities often require architectural changes that take longer to fix than code patches.
The four-tier structure (Critical/High/Medium/Low) with differentiated ACK, verdict, fix, and post-fix windows ensures that engineering urgency is proportionate to actual exploitability and impact.
An extension policy is not optional. AI security fixes will sometimes take longer than the primary SLA window; a published extension process preserves the researcher relationship when this happens.

The researcher's right to disclose after a deadline miss — with appropriate notice — is a commitment to seriousness, not a concession. Publishing this right demonstrates that the organisation treats the VDP as a real governance mechanism.
SLA compliance rate is the primary metric for VDP operational health. Extension rates above 20% for any tier signal miscalibrated commitments that need revision.

Fix safety regression testing for agentic systems must be included in the remediation timeline: a hasty patch that breaks the agent's existing safety controls is worse than a correctly tested fix that takes a few extra days.
